title: HAT Surveillance Methods for Field Epidemiologists in DRC
audience: Field epidemiologists working on gambiense HAT surveillance in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (working knowledge of epidemiology, variable R skills).

modules.title: gHAT and the DRC Surveillance Landscape
objectives: Describe the gHAT transmission cycle and two clinical stages
Explain the DRC burden and the EPHP target
Identify how PNLTHA and the health-zone structure organise surveillance

modules.title: Passive Surveillance and Health-Facility Detection
objectives: Apply the suspect-case definition at a fixed facility
Execute the RDT-to-confirmation algorithm
Calculate passive-detection positivity rate

modules.title: Active Case Finding with Mobile Teams
objectives: Apply risk-targeting logic to choose villages
Plan a screening round
Compute screening coverage and yield

modules.title: Case Definitions, Diagnostic Algorithms, and Test Performance
objectives: Analyse sensitivity and specificity of the gHAT pathway
Calculate PPV and show its prevalence dependence
Justify a multi-step algorithm in an elimination setting

modules.title: Data Flow, DHIS2, and the National HAT Atlas
objectives: Trace the data pipeline from village to DHIS2 and HAT Atlas
Apply data-quality dimensions
Audit a reporting chain

modules.title: Vector Surveillance and Integrated Tsetse Control
objectives: Explain the role of riverine tsetse in gHAT
Apply tiny-target logic to a focus
Design an entomological monitoring plan

modules.title: Analysing and Mapping Surveillance Data in R
objectives: Compute health-zone incidence per 10,000 in R
Produce a time trend
Analyse spatial pattern with a choropleth

modules.title: Surveillance in the Elimination Endgame: Verification and Reintroduction Risk
objectives: Evaluate an evidence package against WHO EPHP criteria
Appraise threats including cryptic transmission and surveillance decay
Judge reintroduction risk and recommend a post-validation posture

retain_long_term: gHAT diagnostic staircase: sensitive screen then specific confirmation; never treat on a screen alone
PPV falls as prevalence falls — at elimination levels most screen-positives are false positives
Screening coverage must be high before low yield is reassuring
Incidence per 10,000 per focus and the EPHP <1/10,000 threshold
flies/trap/day as the entomological monitoring indicator
EPHP requires sustained sub-threshold incidence AND surveillance adequate to prove it
Cryptic transmission and surveillance decay are the two main endgame threats
